context:
Section 8.1 (Formulation), page 28

To understand the state-dependent effect from the first term of (8.2), we examine the (local) dual problem of (8.2), even though strong duality for the full problem over t = 1, . . . , T −1 remains an open question.
title: Strong duality for multi-period equilibrium transport with state-dependent costs
latex:
Given an integer horizon \(T\ge 2\), let \(N\) and \(M\) be finite sets (firm types and worker types). For each \(t\in\{1,\dots,T\}\), let \(n_t\in N\) and \(m_t\in M\). Let \(\mu\) be a probability measure on \(N^T\) and \(\nu\) a probability measure on \(M^T\). Assume \(\mu\) and \(\nu\) are Markov and time-homogeneous: there exist transition kernels \(\mu(\cdot\mid n)\in\mathcal P(N)\) and \(\nu(\cdot\mid m)\in\mathcal P(M)\) such that for all \(t\in\{1,\dots,T-1\}\),
\[
\mu(n_{t+1}\mid n_1,\dots,n_t)=\mu(n_{t+1}\mid n_t),\qquad \nu(m_{t+1}\mid m_1,\dots,m_t)=\nu(m_{t+1}\mid m_t).
\]
Fix parameters \(\alpha\in\mathbb R\), \(\tau>0\), and \(\beta\in(0,1]\). Let \(S:N\to(0,\infty)\) and \(Q:M\to\mathbb R\) be given functions and define the one-period cost \(c:N\times M\to\mathbb R\) by \(c(n,m)=-C_1 S(n)^a Q(m)\) for given constants \(C_1>0\) and \(a>0\). Define the state-dependent one-step term
\[
\ell((n,m),(n',m')):=-\alpha\exp\Big(-\frac{(n'-n)^2+(m'-m)^2}{\tau}\Big).
\]
For each \(t\in\{0,1,\dots,T-1\}\) and state \((n_t,m_t)\in N\times M\), define the equilibrium value function \(V_t:N\times M\to\mathbb R\) by backward recursion with terminal condition \(V_T\equiv 0\) and, for \(t=T-1,\dots,0\),
\[
V_t(n_t,m_t)=\inf_{\gamma\in\Pi(\mu(\cdot\mid n_t),\nu(\cdot\mid m_t))}\n\sum_{(n_{t+1},m_{t+1})\in N\times M}\Big(\ell((n_t,m_t),(n_{t+1},m_{t+1})) + \beta c(n_{t+1},m_{t+1}) + \beta V_{t+1}(n_{t+1},m_{t+1})\Big)\,\gamma(n_{t+1},m_{t+1}),
\]
where \(\Pi(\mu(\cdot\mid n_t),\nu(\cdot\mid m_t))\) denotes the set of all couplings of the marginals \(\mu(\cdot\mid n_t)\) and \(\nu(\cdot\mid m_t)\).

The one-step minimization problems above admit a standard Kantorovich dual formulation for each fixed \(t\) and \((n_t,m_t)\). The problem is to formulate an appropriate global (multi-step) dual problem for the full equilibrium transport over \(t=0,1,\dots,T-1\) (allowing the dual variables to depend on time and state, e.g. via wage/potential functions), and to prove strong duality, i.e. that the equilibrium value \(V_0(n_0,m_0)\) equals the value of this global dual problem and that dual maximizers exist under natural assumptions.
open_problem_quote: To understand the state-dependent effect from the first term of (8.2), we examine the (local) dual problem of (8.2), even though strong duality for the full problem over t = 1, . . . , T −1 remains an open question.
